request.question: What was Alfed Dreyfus lawyer, Fernand Labori a victim of?

title: Fernand Lalonde
Hippolytus de Marsiliis
Fernand Choquette
Alfred Dreyfus
Fernand Dubé
Fernand de Brinon
Fernand Labori
Vera Brühne
Fernand Crémieux
Edgar Demange
sentences: Fernand Lalonde (born August 27, 1932) is a Canadian lawyer and politician.
Lalonde represented the riding of Marguerite-Bourgeoys in the National Assembly of Quebec from 1973 to 1984.
Hippolytus de Marsiliis (born 1451 Bologna; date of death unknown) was a lawyer and "doctor utriusque iuris" (Lat.
'doctor of either law' — one who studied civil as well as canon law).
He received his doctorate in 1480 but the date at which he became a lawyer is unknown.
Throughout his life, he wrote many repetitiones and notabilia on many canons and decretals.
In addition, he taught Roman law beginning in the year 1482.
He is best known for documenting the Chinese water torture method, in which drops of water would consistently fall on a victim's forehead, causing him to go insane.

He also was the first person to document sleep deprivation as a means of torture, wherein the interrogators would repeat same questions, shaking the victim at random intervals, pricking him with a sharp pin, or forcing him to march down a hallway endlessly.
If the interrogators grew weary, they would switch out with another group, who then would ask the same questions (today police use this method, but it is known as the third degree).
Fernand Choquette, {'1': ", '2': ", '3': ", '4': "} (October 27, 1895 – January 17, 1975) was a Canadian Quebec MNA and judge of the Quebec Court of Appeal from 1956 until 1970.
He also worked as a lawyer for over 10 years and a teacher for over 20 years.

Alfred Dreyfus (] ; 9 October 1859 – 12 July 1935) was a French Jewish artillery officer whose trial and conviction in 1894 on charges of treason became one of the most tense political dramas in modern French history with a wide echo in all Europe.
Known today as the Dreyfus affair, the incident eventually ended with Dreyfus's complete exoneration.
Fernand G. Dubé, QC (December 29, 1928 – October 5, 1999) was a Canadian lawyer and politician in the Province of New Brunswick.
Fernand de Brinon, Marquis de Brinon (] ; 26 August 1885 – 15 April 1947) was a French lawyer and journalist who was one of the architects of French collaboration with the Nazis during World War II.
He claimed to have had five private talks with Adolf Hitler between 1933 and 1937.
Fernand-Gustave-Gaston Labori (April 18, 1860 – March 14, 1917) was a French attorney.
He was born in Reims.
In his professional life, he defended the accused in some of the most prominent political cases of his day.
Among his noted clients was Alfred Dreyfus, who was eventually acquitted of treason.
During the Dreyfus trial, Labori was the victim of an assassination attempt which hospitalized him for a week.
The attacker was never identified.
Vera Brühne (February 6, 1910 in Essen – April 17, 2001 in Munich) became famous throughout Germany as a victim of miscarriage of justice.
In 1961/62, she was convicted, together with Johann Ferbach, of having murdered the physician Otto Praun and his lover.
Ulrich Sonnemann considered the case as a new Dreyfus affair.
In 1979, she was pardoned by Bavarian governor Franz Josef Strauss.
Fernand Crémieux (1857-1928) was a French lawyer and politician.
He served as a member of the National Assembly from 1885 to 1889, and from 1893 to 1898, representing Gard.
He also served as a member of the French Senate from 1903 to 1928, representing Gard.
Edgar Demange (April 22, 1841 in Versailles – February 1925 in Paris) was a French jurist.
He was, with Fernand Labori, the lawyer of Alfred Dreyfus during his trials in 1894 and 1899.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.

Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: What was Alfed Dreyfus lawyer, Fernand Labori a victim of?
hop1_docs: Fernand Labori | Fernand-Gustave-Gaston Labori (April 18, 1860 – March 14, 1917) was a French attorney.  He was born in Reims.  In his professional life, he defended the accused in some of the most prominent political cases of his day.  Among his noted clients was Alfred Dreyfus, who was eventually acquitted of treason.  During the Dreyfus trial, Labori was the victim of an assassination attempt which hospitalized him for a week.  The attacker was never identified.

Edgar Demange | Edgar Demange (April 22, 1841 in Versailles – February 1925 in Paris) was a French jurist.  He was, with Fernand Labori, the lawyer of Alfred Dreyfus during his trials in 1894 and 1899.

Mathieu Dreyfus | Mathieu Dreyfus (1857-1930) was an Alsatian Jewish industrialist and the older brother of Alfred Dreyfus, a French military officer falsely convicted of treason in what became known as the Dreyfus Affair.  Mathieu was one of his brother's most loyal supporters throughout the affair.

Ius Laboris | Ius Laboris is an international law firm network specializing in issues relating to employment law, employee benefits, and pensions.  As of 2015, the network had over 1,400 HR lawyers based in around 50 countries across Europe, the Americas, the Middle East, and Asia.
Camille Ferdinand Dreyfus | Ferdinand Dreyfus (5 May 1849 - 15 July 1915) was a wealthy French lawyer, historian and philanthropist who became Senator for Seine-et-Oise from 1909 to 1915.

Édouard Dreyfus Gonzalez | Édouard Vincent Joseph Dreyfus González de Orbegoso, 5th count of Premio Real (1876–1941) was a French Catholic businessman of Jewish descent, a lawyer, painter and writer, who was also an amateur composer under the pseudonym of Jean Dora.

Hubert Dreyfus's views on artificial intelligence | Hubert Dreyfus has been a critic of artificial intelligence research since the 1960s.  In a series of papers and books, including Alchemy and AI (1965), What Computers Can't Do (1972; 1979; 1992) and Mind over Machine (1986), he presented a pessimistic assessment of AI's progress and a critique of the philosophical foundations of the field.  Dreyfus' objections are discussed in most introductions to the philosophy of artificial intelligence, including , the standard AI textbook, and in , a survey of contemporary philosophy.

  2. "Alfred Dreyfus" — inspect 'Trials' (1894, 1899) sections for mention of an attempt on Labori.
  3. "Dreyfus affair" — inspect sections on the trials, public reaction, or violence for corroboration.
  4. Search: "Fernand Labori assassination attempt hospitalized for a week date attacker never identified" for contemporary reports.

Edgar Demange | Edgar Demange (April 22, 1841 in Versailles – February 1925 in Paris) was a French jurist.  He was, with Fernand Labori, the lawyer of Alfred Dreyfus during his trials in 1894 and 1899.

Alfred Dreyfus | Alfred Dreyfus (] ; 9 October 1859 – 12 July 1935) was a French Jewish artillery officer whose trial and conviction in 1894 on charges of treason became one of the most tense political dramas in modern French history with a wide echo in all Europe.  Known today as the Dreyfus affair, the incident eventually ended with Dreyfus's complete exoneration.

Trial and conviction of Alfred Dreyfus | The trial and conviction of Alfred Dreyfus was the event that instigated the Dreyfus Affair, a political scandal which divided France during the 1890s and early 1900s.  It involved the wrongful conviction for treason of Alfred Dreyfus, a French artillery officer of Alsatian Jewish background.  Dreyfus was sentenced to life in prison on Devil's Island.

Fernand Bonnier de La Chapelle | Fernand Bonnier de La Chapelle (1922 – December 26, 1942) was a member of the French resistance during World War II.  He assassinated Admiral of the Fleet François Darlan, the former chief of government of Vichy France and the self-proclaimed high commissioner of French North Africa and West Africa, on December 24, 1942.  Bonnier de La Chapelle's speedy trial and execution fueled theories about who may have been behind the assassination.

The Dreyfus Affair (film series) | The Dreyfus Affair (French: L'affaire Dreyfus ), also known as Dreyfus Court-Martial, is an 1899 series of short silent docudramas, conceived and directed by Georges Méliès.  Released by Méliès's Star Film Company and numbered 206–217 in its catalogs, each of the eleven one-minute installments reconstructs an event from the historical Dreyfus affair, which was still in progress while the series was being made.

Maiden Quest | "Maiden Quest" is the 4th episode of season 5 of the supernatural drama television series "Grimm" and the 92nd episode overall, which premiered on November 20, 2015, on the cable network NBC.  The episode was written by Brenna Kouf and was directed by Hanelle Culpepper.  In the episode, Nick and Hank investigate an assassination attempt on a nightclub owner but they find that this attempt is involved in a trial for a maiden's hand.  Meanwhile, Renard is asked to support the campaign of an old friend for mayor.
